title: Apple

overview.description: A crisp, juicy fruit with a wide range of flavours, colours and textures depending on the variety.

method: Eat it whole, or cut it into slices.
The skin can be eaten, but remove the stalk and pips if cutting it into pieces.

learning.did-you-know: Apples float because approximately a quarter of their volume is made up of tiny air spaces. Apples are also members of the rose family, along with pears, strawberries and cherries.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How apples differ in flavour and texture, how they ripen and why cutting an apple can cause the flesh to turn brown.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A convenient and widely available fruit snack that gives children the opportunity to explore different varieties and learn how the same type of fruit can have very different flavours and textures.
chapman-tips: Different apple varieties can be sweet, sour, crisp, soft or very juicy.
The skin contains fibre and can be eaten.
Apple flesh turns brown after cutting because it reacts with oxygen in the air.
A little lemon juice can slow down browning.
Apples that are less pleasant to eat raw may be excellent for cooking.
notes.leftovers: Apples can be used in crumbles, pies, cakes, sauces, chutneys and salads.
